skill.name: agent-swarm-workflow
skill.description: Guides teams through a parallel AI agent implementation workflow using NTM, Agent Mail, Beads, and BV coordination tools.
skill.summary: Coordinate multiple Claude, Codex, and Claude Code agents through bead-based implementation, review, and commit loops.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-like patterns, but most are markdown examples or workflow prompts rather than executable code. The real risk is operational: the skill directs agents to run local coordination commands, inspect and modify project files, and commit or push changes. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration, hidden network calls, prompt-injection override text, or weak cryptographic API usage.

medium_findings.title: External Command Orchestration Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users and agents to run local tools such as Agent Mail, NTM, BV, jq, and shell command substitution. This is expected for a development workflow, but it can execute commands in a user project and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain direct command examples and command substitution. The usage is legitimate documentation, but it still creates execution risk if followed without review.

medium_findings.title: Autonomous File Modification and Repository Push Guidance
medium_findings.description: Several prompts direct agents to inspect code deeply, fix issues, create tasks, commit grouped changes, and push. This is useful for the intended workflow, but it increases risk of unintended filesystem changes or repository updates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompts explicitly ask agents to modify code and commit or push changes. This is not malicious by itself, but it is a clear operational risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to come from text such as AGENTS dot md and workflow prose, not from cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms being invoked.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows markdown prose and prompt text, not cryptographic APIs. The static pattern is therefore a strong false positive.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Matches Are Workflow Review Prompts
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts map to test coverage, file reservation, and conflict-avoidance workflow text. The skill does encourage codebase exploration, but no evidence found of host fingerprinting or covert system inventory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain workflow guidance, not commands for collecting host or network information. The broader skill still warrants caution because it asks agents to inspect project code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Example Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The NTM examples pass file contents into agent prompts through shell command substitution. Users should inspect prompt files before sending them to multiple agents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit and can transmit arbitrary prompt file content. The example is normal shell usage, so the risk depends on the referenced file contents.

dangerous_patterns.title: Commit and Push Prompt
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes a prompt that asks agents to commit changed files and push. This can affect a remote repository if used without review gates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited prompt directly instructs commit and push behavior. It is intentional workflow guidance rather than hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: Parallel coding agents can conflict, duplicate work, or stall without clear coordination. This skill provides prompts and routines for task selection, Agent Mail coordination, review loops, and grouped commits.
actual_capabilities: Explains prerequisites for a coordinated multi-agent implementation swarm.
Provides startup prompts for registering agents and assigning bead-based work.
Defines review prompts for self-review, cross-agent review, and random code exploration.
Shows Agent Mail patterns for file reservations and progress messages.
Lists BV command examples for selecting next tasks and inspecting graph health.
Includes prompts for test coverage, UI review, context refresh, and commit grouping.
limitations: Requires external tools such as NTM, Agent Mail, Beads, and BV to be installed separately.
Does not implement the coordination tools itself; it only documents workflow prompts and command examples.
Can lead to repository changes or pushes if agents follow prompts without human review.
Assumes a project already has planning artifacts, beads, and an AGENTS.md file.

use_cases.title: Coordinate a Feature Build
use_cases.description: Split a planned feature into bead work and keep several coding agents moving without duplicate edits.

use_cases.title: Run Distributed Code Review
use_cases.description: Ask agents to review their own work and each other's changes before grouped commits.

use_cases.title: Recover Agent Context
use_cases.description: Refresh agents after compaction or crashes so they reread project rules and continue from bead status.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Small Swarm
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a project that already has beads and project instructions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me start a small agent swarm for this repository. Confirm the prerequisites, then prepare the initial marching orders for three agents.

prompt_templates.title: Move Agents to Ready Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate use after one or more agents finish current tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current bead status and draft a prompt that moves agents to the highest-impact ready work while preserving coordination through Agent Mail.

prompt_templates.title: Run Review Rounds
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced quality pass across completed implementation work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a review sequence for self-review, cross-agent review, and random code exploration. Include when each round should stop.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Commit Coordination
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced release preparation after implementation and review loops are complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me organize agent outputs into logical commit groups. Include checks for file reservations, ephemeral files, and human review before any push.

output_examples.input: I have six beads ready and want to use four agents.
output_examples.output: A staged swarm plan with agent startup prompts, task selection guidance, Agent Mail coordination steps, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: An agent completed a bead and is idle.
output_examples.output: A next-task prompt that refreshes project rules, asks the agent to check messages, and directs it to choose useful ready work.

output_examples.input: We finished the first implementation pass.
output_examples.output: A quality loop plan covering self-review, cross-review, random exploration, test coverage review, and commit grouping.

best_practices: Use the workflow only in repositories with clear planning documents, bead status, and project-specific agent rules.
Require file reservations and Agent Mail updates before agents edit shared files.
Review diffs and commit groups manually before allowing agents to push changes.
anti_patterns: Do not start many agents before dependencies, priorities, and file ownership are clear.
Do not let agents commit or push without an explicit review gate.
Do not use broad review prompts as a substitute for project-specific acceptance criteria.

faq.question: Does this skill run the swarm tools for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions, prompts, and command examples for tools you install and run separately.

faq.question: Which tools does the workflow expect?
faq.answer: It expects NTM for sessions, Agent Mail for coordination, Beads for task tracking, and BV for task selection.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code together?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written for mixed agent swarms that may include Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other agents.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains command examples and prompts that can edit files, commit changes, and push repositories if followed directly.

faq.question: Does the skill contain malicious code?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malicious code, data exfiltration, or hidden network behavior in the reviewed SKILL.md file.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use file reservations, keep human review before pushes, and verify every external command before running it in a project.
